my_thoughts: The simplification works: aim by diagnosis (aim = opposite of sleep/mechanicalness), simplifies: aim to binary choice (sleep vs. awake). The student asks aim question (seeking aim formulation), which shows they understand: aim = needs formulation (methodology). They'll likely identify core problem next. Hypothesis: aim simplifications trigger problem identifications—testable by tracking whether aim simplifications lead to problem identifications.
accumulated_insight: T1: Student asks aim question ('How can a man who realizes his position formulate his aim?')—seeking aim formulation via asks for methodology and seeks aim criterion. They understand: aim = needs formulation (methodology). The comparison_contrast mechanism defines: aim by diagnosis (aim = opposite of sleep/mechanicalness), simplifies: aim to binary choice (sleep vs. awake). Pattern: aim question → simplification → [next: problem identification]. Low confidence because simplifications can lead to deeper questions or problem identifications.
visible_response:
He will see that
the centre of gravity of his position is sleep;
then his aim will be to awake.
Or if he sees his mechanicalness, his aim will be to get
rid of mechanicalness.
Both come to the same thing.
